title: Research
text:
My research examines how scientific knowledge is produced through code, statistical workflows, and research infrastructures, with the goal of improving the credibility and transparency of computational science. I develop theoretical frameworks and evaluation tools for assessing research code, including rubric-based approaches for evaluating reproducible statistical workflows. My broader program advances a systems-level approach to scientific practice, integrating computational epistemology, evaluation theory, and meta-science.

title: Marcus Harris
text:
I build tools and theories for reliable measurement in psychology and education. My work focuses on psychometric modeling, meta-research, and open, reproducible methods.
Profile - View my profile
Research Focus - Measurement invariance and fairness - Meta-analytic methods and bias detection - Bayesian and multilevel psychometrics
Current Work - Developing simulation tools for power and reliability in scale validation - Auditing common practices in scale development across subfields - Building open teaching materials for measurement theory
Essays - Read the essays and blog
CV - View the CV page - Download CV (PDF)
Contact - Email: marcus.harris@uconn.edu - GitHub: https://github.com/MarcusHarrisUConn

title: On Measurement as Infrastructure
text:
Measurement is not a sidebar to science�it is the infrastructure that makes discovery legible. When we get measurement wrong, we silently corrupt every downstream inference.
This essay outlines a pragmatic view of measurement: treat it as a public good, document its assumptions, and design it for reuse.

title: Profile
text:
Marcus Harris is a PhD researcher in research methods, measurement, and evaluation whose work sits at the intersection of psychology, meta-research, and computational reproducibility. His research examines how scientific knowledge is produced through code, statistical workflows, and research infrastructures, with the goal of improving the credibility and transparency of computational science. He develops theoretical frameworks and evaluation tools for assessing research code, including rubric-based approaches for evaluating reproducible statistical workflows. His broader program of research advances a systems-level approach to scientific practice, integrating computational epistemology, evaluation theory, and meta-science to design more trustworthy research ecosystems.
Contact - Email: marcus.harris@uconn.edu - GitHub: https://github.com/MarcusHarrisUConn
